findings.description: reachable() has no visited-guard before enqueueing neighbors, so on the cyclic graphs its own docstring promises to support, the queue never drains and it loops forever / exhausts memory (e.g. edges a->b, b->a).

Even on an acyclic graph reachable() re-enqueues already-seen nodes (no dedup before append), causing exponential queue growth on diamond/DAG shapes — a denial-of-service-grade blowup, not just slowness.

reachable() never adds `start` to `seen`, so a node reachable from itself only via a cycle is reported but a plain start is omitted; the set is inconsistent with the 'all nodes reachable from start' contract (start is reachable from itself at distance 0).

Reads of self._edges[node] (here and in count_paths/topological_order/neighbors) mutate the defaultdict by inserting empty lists for missing keys, so read-only traversals silently grow internal state and desync _edges from _nodes.

neighbors() (and reachable) on an unknown node returns [] while silently creating that key in _edges via defaultdict access, masking caller bugs and registering a node in _edges that node_count()/_nodes never sees.
count_paths() recurses unconditionally with no cycle guard or memoization; on any cyclic input it hits RecursionError (the 'DAG assumed' contract is undefended) and on a wide DAG it is exponential.
count_paths() counts parallel/duplicate edges as distinct paths because add_edge permits multi-edges, so the 'number of distinct paths' result is inflated for multigraphs.
add_edge() accepts self-loops (add_edge(x,x)), which silently make topological_order() return None and make reachable()/count_paths() loop or recurse infinitely, with no validation or warning.

topological_order() seeds and traverses from a set (_nodes), so among multiple valid orderings the chosen one is non-deterministic across runs due to set/hash ordering; if callers expect a stable order this is a latent contract gap.
add_node() logs at INFO on every call while add_edge() logs nothing, an inconsistent/noisy logging contract that will flood logs in bulk-load pipelines.
